squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Ancelotti's squad; Neymar recalled after 2.5 years, Rodrygo/Militao/Richarlison omitted. Marquinhos + Casemiro co-captains.

squads.name: Argentina
squads.notes: Scaloni keeps the 2022-winning core; Acuna and Mastantuono omitted. Messi's sixth World Cup.

squads.name: France
squads.notes: Deschamps' final squad before stepping down; Camavinga and Griezmann (retired) absent. Mbappe captains his third World Cup.

squads.name: England
squads.notes: Tuchel's squad: shock omissions of Foden, Palmer, Alexander-Arnold and Maguire. Kane captains his third World Cup.

squads.name: Spain
squads.notes: De la Fuente's squad — for the first time no Real Madrid player is included; Rodri captains. La Masia spine of eight Barca players.

squads.name: Germany
squads.notes: Nagelsmann's squad; 40-year-old Neuer reversed retirement to be first choice, Kimmich captains, Wirtz at his first World Cup.

squads.name: Portugal
squads.notes: Roberto Martinez's squad; Cristiano Ronaldo captains a record sixth World Cup. A symbolic place was dedicated to the late Diogo Jota.

squads.name: Netherlands
squads.notes: Koeman's Premier-League-heavy squad; Xavi Simons ruled out with an ACL rupture, Frimpong a notable absentee. Van Dijk captains.

squads.name: Belgium
squads.notes: Rudi Garcia's rebuilt squad; Tielemans captains around De Bruyne, Lukaku and Courtois. Faes/Openda/Bakayoko among those dropped.

squads.name: Croatia
squads.notes: Dalic's blend of veterans and youth; Modric (40) captains his fifth World Cup, Gvardiol back from injury as defensive anchor.

squads.name: Morocco
squads.notes: Mohamed Ouahbi's squad (replaced Regragui); nine 2022 semi-finalists return led by Hakimi, Bounou and Amrabat. En-Nesyri, Boufal and Ziyech cut; 18-year-old Bouaddi debuts.

squads.name: Japan
squads.notes: Moriyasu's squad for an eighth straight World Cup; Endo captains and Mitoma misses out with a hamstring injury. Kubo is the main creative outlet.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Scolari's 3-5-2 with the '3R' (Ronaldo-Rivaldo-Ronaldinho). Ronaldo's redemption tournament: 8 goals, Golden Boot.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Zagallo's side, widely called the greatest team ever. 4-3-3 with Pelé in a free role; Jairzinho scored in every match.

squads.name: Argentina
squads.notes: Bilardo's 3-5-2, carried by Maradona's greatest tournament. Squad numbered alphabetically (Pumpido, the #1 keeper, wore 18).

squads.name: France
squads.notes: Hosts and champions; Zidane's coming-out (two headers in the final). Barthez started in #16 — the #1 shirt went to Lama.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Parreira's pragmatic 4-4-2 won on penalties. Romário's Golden Ball tournament; a 17-year-old Ronaldo travelled but never played.

squads.name: Germany
squads.notes: Löw's champions — a team of stars with no single superstar. Götze's extra-time volley beat Argentina; Klose became the World Cup's all-time top scorer.

squads.name: Spain
squads.notes: Del Bosque's tiki-taka. Iniesta's 116th-minute goal won the final; Xavi orchestrated from midfield.

squads.name: Netherlands
squads.notes: Michels' Total Football, fluid 4-3-3 orbiting Cruyff's free role. Shirts numbered alphabetically — Cruyff kept his famous 14, keeper Jongbloed wore 8.

squads.name: Argentina
squads.notes: Scaloni's champions — Messi's crowning tournament (Golden Ball, 7 goals). Beat France on penalties after a 3-3 final.

squads.name: Italy
squads.notes: Lippi's champions, built on the best defense in the world. Cannavaro won the Ballon d'Or; Grosso's penalty settled the final vs France.

squads.name: Croatia
squads.notes: Dalić's side rode Modrić's Golden Ball / Ballon d'Or peak to the final, winning three straight knockout games in extra time before losing 4-2 to France.

squads.name: France
squads.notes: Deschamps' young champions; a 19-year-old Mbappé and Griezmann both scored in a 4-2 final over Croatia. Kanté + Pogba anchored midfield.

squads.name: Portugal
squads.notes: Scolari's semi-finalists. A 21-year-old Cristiano Ronaldo played as a winger (#17), years before his goal-machine era; Deco and captain Figo ran midfield.

squads.name: England
squads.notes: Southgate's semi-finalists in a 3-5-2 with attacking wing-backs. Kane won the Golden Boot (6 goals); Trippier's free-kick opened the semi-final vs Croatia.

squads.name: Morocco
squads.notes: Regragui's history-makers — first African/Arab World Cup semi-finalists. Bounou saved two penalties in the shootout that eliminated Spain; Amrabat anchored a 4-1-4-1.

squads.name: Uruguay
squads.notes: Hosts and champions of the first-ever World Cup, beating Argentina 4-2 in the final. Nasazzi captained; Castro scored the winner with his one arm.

squads.name: Uruguay
squads.notes: The Maracanazo: beat host Brazil 2-1 in front of ~200,000 in the deciding match of the final group. Schiaffino equalized, Ghiggia won it. Varela captained.

squads.name: West Germany
squads.notes: The Miracle of Bern: beat Hungary 3-2 in the final after losing 3-8 to them in the group stage. Rahn scored the winner; Fritz Walter captained.

squads.name: Hungary
squads.notes: The Mighty Magyars — unbeaten for over 4 years, hammered England 6-3 and 7-1, beat this same West German side 8-3 in the group. Lost only the final, 2-3. Often called the greatest team never to win the World Cup.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Brazil's first title, in Sweden. A 17-year-old Pele scored a hat-trick in the semifinal and two in the final; Garrincha and Pele broke through together from the third group match, birthing the legendary 4-2-4. Didi won the Golden Ball.

squads.name: England
squads.notes: Hosts and champions, England's only World Cup win. Ramsey's 'Wingless Wonders' 4-4-2; Hurst's hat-trick beat West Germany 4-2 in the final at Wembley, still the only World Cup final hat-trick.

squads.name: West Germany
squads.notes: Beckenbauer's champions beat the Netherlands 2-1 in the final, completing the other half of that story (see NED 1974). Vogts man-marked Cruyff out of the game; Muller scored the winner.

squads.name: Argentina
squads.notes: Menotti's home-soil champions beat the Netherlands 3-1 (a.e.t.) in the final. Kempes scored twice in the final and finished top scorer of the tournament; Passarella captained.

squads.name: Italy
squads.notes: Bearzot's champions, beaten 3-1 over West Germany in the final. Paolo Rossi, back from a match-fixing ban, scored a hat-trick vs Brazil and won both the Golden Boot and Golden Ball. Zoff (40) captained.

squads.name: West Germany
squads.notes: Beckenbauer coached his old team to the title, beating Argentina 1-0 in a final marred by red cards. Matthaus captained and won the Ballon d'Or that year; Brehme scored the winning penalty.

squads.name: Italy
squads.notes: Hosts and first-ever champions under Vittorio Pozzo, beating Czechoslovakia 2-1 (a.e.t.). Orsi's swerving equalizer and Schiavio's winner sealed it. Luis Monti became the only man to play in World Cup finals for two different countries (Argentina 1930, Italy 1934).

squads.name: Italy
squads.notes: Pozzo's back-to-back champions, beating Hungary 4-2 in the final. Piola and Colaussi each scored twice; Meazza captained again. The only nation to retain the World Cup until Brazil matched it in 1962.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Back-to-back champions, matching Italy's feat, beating Czechoslovakia 3-1. Pele injured after match two; Amarildo stepped in and starred. Garrincha carried the team and won the Golden Ball - widely considered his single greatest tournament.

squads.name: Sweden
squads.notes: Hosts, lost the final 5-2 to Pele's Brazil. Built around the aging 'Gre-No-Li' pair (Gren and Liedholm, reunited from their AC Milan trio) plus young winger Hamrin. Squad depth below the front-line names is less firmly documented.

squads.name: Netherlands
squads.notes: Runners-up again, this time without Cruyff (he opted out). Krol captained. Rensenbrink hit the post in the final's last minute of normal time before Nanninga's late equalizer forced extra time; Argentina won it there. Pairs with the existing Argentina 1978 squad.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: Telê Santana's team - often called the greatest side never to win the World Cup. Eliminated by Rossi's hat-trick for Italy, 3-2, in one of the classic matches in World Cup history. Pairs with the existing Italy 1982 squad.

squads.name: Cameroon
squads.notes: The Indomitable Lions - first African team to reach a World Cup quarterfinal. Beat defending champions Argentina 1-0 in the opener; 38-year-old Roger Milla came off the bench to score four goals and dance at the corner flag, becoming a global icon. Lost the QF to England 3-2 (a.e.t.).

squads.name: United States
squads.notes: Hosts, made the knockout stage in their own tournament before losing 1-0 to eventual champions Brazil. Beat Colombia 2-1 in the group stage (Stewart's winner, the match remembered for Escobar's own goal). Verified names/numbers from the squad template; positions/clubs are not in that source, so authored from established US soccer history and omitted where uncertain.

squads.name: South Korea
squads.notes: Guus Hiddink's co-host semi-finalists - the biggest shock of the modern World Cup era, beating Portugal, Italy, and Spain (on penalties) before losing to Germany. Hong Myung-bo captained; Ahn Jung-hwan's golden goal beat Italy; Park Ji-sung broke through here.

squads.name: Colombia
squads.notes: Jose Pekerman's team, Colombia's best World Cup run - lost 2-1 to hosts Brazil in the QF. James Rodriguez won the Golden Boot with 6 goals, including a scissor-kick volley vs Uruguay in the Round of 16 widely rated among the greatest World Cup goals ever. Falcao missed the tournament through injury.

squads.name: Portugal
squads.notes: Santos' side a year after the Euro triumph; a mid-tier team carried by a peak Ronaldo (hat-trick vs Spain). Knocked out 2-1 by Uruguay.

squads.name: Belgium
squads.notes: The golden generation's peak: a 3-0 semi-final run ended by France, then bronze. De Bruyne and Hazard at their zenith, Courtois won the Golden Glove.

squads.name: France
squads.notes: Deschamps' defending champions; Mbappé's final hat-trick couldn't stop Argentina on penalties. Griezmann outstanding in a deeper role.

squads.name: Argentina
squads.notes: Sabella's side reached the final on a peak Messi and a colossal Mascherano; beaten 1-0 by Germany in extra time. Di María injured before the final.

squads.name: Germany
squads.notes: Löw's young, thrilling side thrashed England 4-1 and Argentina 4-0 before losing to Spain; Müller's five goals took the Golden Boot.

squads.name: Netherlands
squads.notes: Van Marwijk's pragmatic Oranje won all six matches to the final, where Spain beat them 1-0 in extra time. Sneijder and Robben drove the run.

squads.name: Italy
squads.notes: Sacchi's Italy, dragged to the final by a limping Roberto Baggio, lost the first World Cup decided on penalties — Baggio ballooning the last kick over the bar.

squads.name: Brazil
squads.notes: The 'magic quartet' of Ronaldinho, Kaká, Ronaldo and Adriano flattered to deceive; Zidane's France knocked them out in the quarters.

squads.name: Uruguay
squads.notes: Tabárez's revival reached the semis on Forlán's Golden Ball and Suárez's goal-line handball vs Ghana; La Celeste's best finish since 1970.

squads.name: England
squads.notes: Ramsey's holders, rated stronger than the 1966 winners, lost 3-2 to West Germany after leading 2-0 — with Banks struck down by illness and Bonetti deputising.
